Wastewater Treatment - Unified Modeling Report

Generated 2026-05-16 14:35  ·  Experiments 1-5  ·  Neural Networks & Ensembles  ·  Feature Engineering  ·  Temporal Features  ·  9 models  ·  8 effluent targets  ·  Train 2021-2024 · Test 2025

Overview

Abstract

  • Baseline (Exp1 & 2): Established that inlet water quality alone is insufficient for prediction. Secondary clarifier data is essential to achieve meaningful accuracy.
  • Feature Expansion (Exp3): Aeration basin data (Exp3-SE2) produced the best linearly-regularised models (ElasticNet/Ridge), providing the most robust baseline predicting 2025 holdout data.
  • Feature Pruning (Exp4): Removing collinear features (via VIF or manually) degraded generalisation across the board. The collinear features carry structural signal necessary for decision tree models, while regularised linear models already suppress collinearity automatically.
  • Advanced Methods (Ensembles & Temporal): Stacking and Neural Networks (ANN) struggled to generalise on the extremely small Composite datasets (n≈290). Conversely, temporal lag features successfully improved R² on Grab targets, demonstrating that recent flow history strongly influences spot samples.
  • Practical significance - R² alone is insufficient: A high Test R² does not guarantee compliance-grade accuracy. Discharge limits for BOD and TSS are 10 mg/L. Grab BOD (best R²≈0.69): RMSE ≈ 2 mg/L - roughly 20% of the limit, operationally useful. Grab TSS (best R²≈0.64): RMSE ≈ 6 mg/L - roughly 60% of the limit; the model explains variance well but its absolute prediction error is large relative to the compliance threshold. Predictions should be treated as trend indicators rather than compliance certifications for TSS.

Global Leaderboard - Best Result Per Target

Left half: the highest raw Test R² achieved for each target across all experiments - the naive ceiling. A ⚠ superscript on the Test R² value flags targets where the naive champion is overfit (gap ≥ 0.10). Right half: the gap-adjusted recommendation - the best model after penalising large train/test gaps (see Model Selection for the full rule explanation).

Target Naive champion (highest Test R²) Recommended (gap-adjusted) Compliance Error (Rec. Model)
ModelTest R²R² GapRMSEExperiment Model · ExperimentR²GapRMSE LimitRMSE % LimitMdAE % Limit
Grab BODVoting0.674-0.1362.269Phase 10 - FE (all)10 mg/L22.7%-
Grab CODXGB0.546 ⚠0.37414.436Exp5 SE2-FS - Grab + Composite Inlet (FS)Exp3-S2-FS · XGB0.493-0.05116.282250 mg/L6.5%-
Grab TSSVoting0.642-0.0836.009Phase 10 - FE (all)10 mg/L60.1%-
Grab pHRF0.484 ⚠0.2820.117Exp3 SE4 - All Featuresno better alternative foundⓘ

1 model fall within the ±0.05 R² competitive window: Exp3-S4 · RF (R²=0.484, gap=+0.282). The smallest |gap| among them is +0.282 - every competitive model carries a substantial overfit gap. The gap-adjusted score (max 0.393) still peaks at the naive champion (Exp3-S4 · RF), so no less-overfit alternative achieves comparable accuracy on this target. This target needs more training data, better features, or a regime-specific model rather than a different model from the current pool.

MdAE % Limit is computed from row-level predictions stored in dataset files. Feature Engineering and Temporal Features phases do not store row-level predictions, so MdAE shows - when their models are the recommended choice. BOD/TSS targets only - MdAE is not shown for pH (narrow range) or COD (no discharge limit).

Metric Progression Across Experiments

View how model performance evolved across experiments for specific targets. Gaps in a line indicate the model was not run in that experiment. R²: higher is better. RMSE / MAE: lower is better (original units, mg/L or pH).

Experiment 1 - Inlet Features

Experiment 1 investigates how far inlet-only features can predict effluent quality. SE1 uses only the 4 core inlet concentrations - the absolute floor. SE2 adds Flow, Power, and calendar features (sin/cos projections for month and day-of-week) raising the count to 11. SE3 extends to all 9 available extended inlet measurements plus COMMON (16 features total), covering both grab and composite targets. Training rows are limited by joint missingness of the supplementary inlet columns (TKN/NH3-N, O&G, PO4, Total/Fecal Coliform) - see Findings Q1 for current row counts. Feature selection in SE3-FS is model-specific: OLS uses LassoCV, Ridge uses the full set, ElNet selects via L1, and trees use OOF permutation importance (threshold 5%).

▶ SE1 - Core Inlet Only
Feature Set
Core Inlet (4 features) - no process or temporal context
Columns:
  • Features (4): Inlet pH, Inlet BOD (mg/L), Inlet COD (mg/L), Inlet TSS (mg/L) - Grab or Composite.
  • Excluded: Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), all calendar features - no process or temporal context.
Hypothesis: Absolute minimum feature set: can inlet water quality alone predict effluent quality? This sub-experiment establishes the floor and quantifies how much process context (Flow, Power, calendar) adds when compared against Exp1 (Sub 2).

▶ SE2 - Inlet + COMMON
Feature Set
Core Inlet + COMMON (11 features) - standard SE2
Columns:
  • Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS - Grab or Composite.
  • COMMON (7): Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), year, month_sin, month_cos, dow_sin, dow_cos.
  • Calendar encoding: month and day_of_week replaced by sin/cos projections - the project standard from SE2 onwards.
Hypothesis: Project-standard inlet feature set. Establishes the inlet-only ceiling before adding supplementary inlet measurements in SE3.

▶ SE3 - Extended Inlet + COMMON (Full + FS)
▶ Full Feature Set
Feature Set
Extended Inlet + COMMON (16 features) - grab and composite targets
Columns:
  • Core Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS (Grab).
  • Extended Inlet (5): TKN/NH3-N, Oil & Grease, PO4/TP, Total Coliform, Fecal Coliform. Note: TKN/NH3-N and O&G are measured on composite Raw Sewage samples (2022+); on the single grab sample in 2021. Column header changed from TKN to NH3-N in 2022 - these are related but distinct nitrogen parameters. No separate Grab equivalents exist for TKN/NH3-N or O&G.
  • COMMON (7): Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), year, month_sin, month_cos, dow_sin, dow_cos.
  • 8 datasets: 4 grab + 4 composite targets. Train row counts limited by joint missingness of the 5 supplementary columns - see Findings Q1.
Hypothesis: Tests whether the full set of available inlet measurements - including microbiological and nutrient indicators - adds predictive value beyond the 4 core inlet parameters. Row count is an inherent data limitation driven by supplementary column missingness, not a design choice.
▶ Dataset Details (n_train / n_test / n_features)

▶ Feature Selection
Feature Set
All Grab Inlet + COMMON - Feature Selected (2-15 features)
Columns:
  • Starting set: SE3 features (15: 9 inlet + 7 COMMON - grab targets only).
  • OLS: LassoCV pre-screen. Ridge: full set (L2). ElNet: full set (L1+L2 selects internally).
  • Trees: 3-phase OOF permutation importance (threshold 5%).
  • Remaining: 2-15 features per model/target.
Hypothesis: Checks whether removing noisy or redundant supplementary inlet features from the SE3 set recovers generalisation on the 2025 holdout.
▶ Dataset Details - Per-Model (n_train / n_test / n_features selected)

Model-specific feature selection: each model may select a different number of features. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen; ElNet selects via internal L1; RF/GB/XGB use OOF permutation importance. n_features shown = selected count per model.

Test R²: ≥0.6 strong · 0.4-0.6 good · 0.2-0.4 moderate · 0-0.2 weak · <0 fails baseline. Best per row: ★ highest Test R².

R² Gap (Train − Test): ■ <0.10 OK · ■ 0.10-0.25 mild overfit · ■ >0.25 severe overfit - treat result with caution.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

Inline deltas are shown for feature-selected variants only. For Test R², positive Δ means the FS model improved. For Gap, RMSE, MAE, and MdAE, positive Δ means the metric decreased versus the full-feature baseline.

▶ Comparisons

★ = best raw Test R² per target · ✦ = best gap-adjusted per target (shown only when raw winner has |gap| > 0.10) · green Δ = improvement · red Δ = regression. RMSE reported in native units (mg/L or pH units). Gap = Train R² - Test R²; red Gap > 0.10 = notable overfit.

Feature Selection Validation - SE3-FS (15 features input, grab targets only)

Each model applies a distinct selection protocol to the shared 15-feature pool. The table confirms which method was used, how many features were retained on average across the 4 grab targets, and whether the protocol was correctly applied.

Model FS Method Input
Feats		 Avg Selected
(across 8 targets)		 Status
OLSLassoCV - L1 cross-validated pre-screen (TimeSeriesSplit)1515.0 / 15Selection applied
RidgeFull set - L2 regularisation handles collinearity internally1515 / 15 (all)Correctly bypassed (L2)
ElNetInternal L1 - retains features with non-zero fitted coefficients155.5 / 15Selection applied
RFOOF permutation importance >= 5% threshold (3-phase: full -> select -> refit)154.2 / 15Selection applied
GBOOF permutation importance >= 5% threshold (3-phase: full -> select -> refit)156.2 / 15Selection applied
XGBOOF permutation importance >= 5% threshold (3-phase: full -> select -> refit)156.8 / 15Selection applied

Ridge is the only model that intentionally bypasses feature removal - L2 shrinks uninformative coefficients toward zero without discarding them. All other models reduce the feature space. Per-target selected feature lists are in the Model-Specific Feature Selection fold within SE3 above.

Transition Summary

Transition Net Mean ΔR²
raw		 Improvements
Δ > +0.01		 Regressions
Δ < −0.01		 Negligible
|Δ| ≤ 0.01		 Gap-Adj Net ΔR²
R²−0.5·max(0,|gap|−0.10)		 Gap-Adj − Raw
overfitting shift		 Verdict / Interpretation
SE1 → SE2-0.726122/48 (avg +0.314)21/48 (avg -1.989)5/48-1.1207-0.3946Net regression
Raw gains partially inflated by increased overfitting
SE2 → SE3-Full-0.051420/48 (avg +1.443)28/48 (avg -1.119)0/48+0.0789+0.1303Net regression
Raw gains understated - overfitting decreased
SE3-Full → SE3-FS-0.97946/48 (avg +0.852)19/48 (avg -2.743)23/48-1.6908-0.7114Net regression
Raw gains partially inflated by increased overfitting

Reading the table. Net Mean ΔR² is the signed average across all model x target cells. SE2 = Core Inlet + COMMON (11 features: 4 inlet + 7 COMMON; grab and composite targets). SE3-Full = 15-feature extended inlet set (grab and composite targets, ~295 grab / ~281 composite train rows). SE3-FS = SE3 with model-specific selection applied (OLS: LassoCV; Ridge: full set L2; ElNet: full set L1+L2; RF/GB/XGB: OOF perm-imp refit). Gap-Adj - Raw indicates whether raw gains are inflated by increased overfitting (negative = gains overstated) or understated by reduced overfitting (positive = gains understated). ★ = best raw Test R² per target · ✦ = best gap-adjusted per target (shown only when raw winner has |gap| > 0.10). In the delta columns: green ΔR² = improvement · red ΔR² = regression · red ΔGap = gap widened (more overfit) · green ΔGap = gap narrowed (less overfit) · grey = negligible (|Δ| ≤ 0.01).

▶ Findings - Experiment 1
Q1 Do inlet concentrations alone carry meaningful predictive power?

It depends on which inlet features are used. With only the 4 core inlet concentrations (SE1): barely. Only 6/48 (model x target) cells achieved positive Test R². Best SE1 result: OLS on Grab BOD (R² = +0.185). Mean Test R² across SE1: -0.879. All composite targets and Grab COD returned universally negative R², establishing that 4 bare inlet features are not sufficient on their own.
SE3 does not improve on SE1/SE2: expanding to all available inlet measurements (grab and composite targets, ~295 grab / ~281 composite train rows) yields near-universal negative Test R² (best = +0.066). The supplementary inlet columns introduce a non-stationarity problem driven by anomalous 2022 readings. See Q6 for the full diagnosis.

Q2 What does adding process context (Flow, Power, cyclic calendar) contribute?

Both model families degraded. Adding 7 process/calendar features increased parameter count without proportional signal gain at this dataset size - OLS especially suffers from overfitting with 11 unconstrained coefficients.
Linear models (SE2): mean Δ = -1.154 overall (Grab: -0.260, Composite: -2.047).
Tree models: mean Δ = -0.299 overall (Grab: -0.012, Composite: -0.585). Ridge and ElNet are the meaningful linear references here - OLS without regularisation is poorly suited to 11 features and its column in the comparison table largely reflects overfitting, not the actual signal in the feature set.

Q3 Does model-specific feature selection improve accuracy or generalisation within SE3?

Model-specific FS reduced mean Test R² on grab targets (10/24 cells regressed).
Grab targets: mean delta = -0.388 (linear: +0.000, trees: -0.777).
Generalisation: Mean R²-gap change (FS - Full) = +0.387 (3/24 model-target pairs saw the gap narrow).
Note: SE3 has ~295 grab / ~281 composite train rows. At this dataset size, OOF FS selects 2-10 features per model/target - the supplementary inlet columns (TKN, O&G, PO4, Coliforms) are frequently dropped, suggesting their individual signal is weak relative to the core 4 inlet features.

Q4 Which variant achieves the best performance per target?

Raw winner: SE2 (1 SE1 / 6 SE2 / 0 SE3-Full / 1 SE3-FS stars). Gap-adjusted winner: SE2 (2 SE1 / 5 SE2 / 0 SE3-Full / 1 SE3-FS diamonds). SE3/SE3-FS cover grab and composite targets (~295 grab / ~281 composite train rows). The table below shows the best raw Test R² per variant per target:

★ = raw R² winner · ✦ = gap-adj winner · green = both · orange = raw only · blue = gap-adj only. Small grey value = gap-adjusted score. Dash (-) = no result for that SE.

Q5 Do Grab and Composite targets respond differently to feature changes?

Yes, for SE1→SE2. Grab targets respond more positively than composite.
SE1 → SE2: Grab -0.136, Composite -1.316. SE3: SE3 now includes both grab (295 train) and composite (281 train) datasets. Best grab R²_test = +0.066, best composite R²_test = -0.934. Both are near-zero or negative; the performance gap between grab and composite is not meaningful when neither generalises. The structural gap between grab and composite persists in SE2 and earlier SEs; secondary treatment data is needed for composite targets (Experiment 2 and beyond).

Q6 Why was Grab SE3 retired, and what does the measurement-type correction reveal?

Grab SE3 was retired due to a measurement-type correction that eliminated all grab-sourced extended features from the 2025 test set.

What was discovered. Frequency analysis of the raw lab reports showed that the extended inlet features switched from grab to composite measurement in two waves: TKN and O&G became composite in January 2022 (~22-24 readings/month vs ~8-9/month in 2021); PO4/TP and both Coliform measures became composite in 2023 (~29 readings/month vs ~4-8/month in 2021-2022). The extraction script previously scanned grab and composite sub-blocks together, silently placing composite-sourced values into grab-labelled columns. The corrected extraction now maintains separate grab and composite columns for each extended feature (see table below).

Why grab SE3 cannot be evaluated. After applying the correction, every grab-sourced extended feature has zero rows in 2024 and zero rows in the 2025 test set. A dropna() on any grab feature set that includes extended features produces an empty 2025 holdout - the model cannot be scored. SE2 (no extended features) gives 1175 train / 218 test rows with full 2021-2024 coverage; adding any grab-sourced extended feature drops the test set to zero (second table below).

What SE3 becomes. SE3 is restructured as composite-only: 4 composite target datasets using COMP_INLET (4) + NH3-N Composite + O&G Composite + COMMON (7) = 13 features. NH3-N and O&G composite are densely covered from 2022 (~1298 and ~1268 rows respectively); PO4 and Coliform composite are excluded because adding PO4 composite halves training rows (737 to 340) due to its absence before mid-2023. The composite SE3 datasets have ~737-738 train rows and ~213-216 test rows.

Extended inlet feature 20212022202320242025 Measurement type
TKN50----grab through 2021
O&G51----grab through 2021
PO4/TP163389--grab through 2023
Total Coliform3367179--grab through 2023
Fecal Coliform3367144--grab through 2023

Green = grab-sourced rows (correct for grab prediction). Red = composite-sourced values stored in grab column (incorrect - would mix measurement types). Dash = no data that year.

Grab feature set (core inlet + COMMON + ...) n train 2024 in train 2025 test rows
SE2 baseline (no extended features)1175296218
+ Total Coliform (grab)241-0 - no test data
+ Fecal Coliform (grab)222-0 - no test data
+ Total + Fecal Coliform (grab)222-0 - no test data
+ PO4/TP (grab)87-0 - no test data
+ PO4 + Total + Fecal Coliform (grab)84-0 - no test data
+ TKN (grab)47-0 - no test data
+ O&G (grab) [uses Inlet O&G (mg/L, Grab)]47-0 - no test data

Representative target: Effluent BOD (Grab). Results are identical for TSS, COD, pH. Red test column = model cannot be evaluated (empty holdout set).

Best Model in Experiment 1 avg best Test R² = -0.275 · Grab avg -0.031 · Composite avg -0.520
TargetModel Test R² GapSource (gap-adj winner)
Grab BODOLS+0.185-0.144SE1 (4 feat · core inlet)

raw: XGB -0.057 (SE2 · XGB (11 feat))
Grab TSSRidge+0.107-0.095SE2 · Ridge (11 feat)

Comp BODElNet-0.159+0.194SE2 · ElNet (11 feat)

⚠ Overfitting note: Grab pH, Comp COD, Comp TSS, Comp pH show |gap| > 0.25 even on the gap-adj winner - treat these results with caution.

Selection: winner chosen by gap-adjusted score = R² − 0.5 · max(0, |gap| − 0.10), consistent with the global leaderboard methodology. Where the raw R² winner differs from the gap-adj winner it is shown in orange below the source label. avg best Test R² uses the raw per-target best for cross-experiment comparability. Composite averages should be read cautiously - all composite targets fail at the Exp1 feature level (Grab COD likewise); secondary clarifier data is required before these targets become learnable.

Experiment 2 - Stage-by-Stage Feature Expansion

Experiment 2 steps through the treatment plant stage by stage, starting from where Experiment 1 left off. SE1 tests the primary clarification and grit removal stage in two sub-scopes to isolate whether primary features carry any standalone signal: SE1a (Primary + COMMON, 13 features) excludes inlet entirely; SE1b (Primary + Inlet + COMMON, 17 features) adds core inlet as a paired comparison. Both sub-scopes produce all-negative R², confirming primary features are genuinely non-predictive regardless of whether inlet is included. SE2 replaces the primary group with secondary treatment data, tested in three scopes: Sec Clarifier alone (10 features), Sec Sedimentation alone (10 features), and both combined (15 features). The split variants establish whether the two secondary sub-groups carry distinct signal or are interchangeable. SE3 combines all three monitoring points - inlet, primary, and secondary - into a single feature set (27 features). The CIS baseline (Core Inlet + Secondary + Common, 21 features) is retained for direct delta comparison.

▶ SE1 - Primary Stage Features (two sub-scopes)

Result: primary-stage scopes are strongly negative on average, with only isolated positive cells. Two scopes are tested to isolate whether the failure is due to primary features themselves or inlet contamination:
• SE1a (Primary + COMMON, 13 feat) - inlet excluded. If primary carries any standalone signal, it should appear here.
• SE1b (Primary + Inlet + COMMON, 17 feat) - adds core inlet (4 features). Provides the paired comparison against SE1a.
Both sub-scopes produce strongly negative aggregate R², confirming that primary features are genuinely weak predictors - the failure is not caused by inlet collinearity alone. OLS is additionally compromised by extreme collinearity (Primary Clarifier pH VIF ~2743 vs Inlet pH). Conclusion: primary-stage measurements carry no standalone predictive signal. Secondary data is needed - see SE2.

▶ SE1a - Primary + COMMON only (13 features)
Feature Set
Primary Stage + COMMON only (13 features)
Columns:
  • Primary Clarifier (5): Primary Clarifier pH, Primary TSS, Primary BOD, Primary COD, Primary Sludge Totalizer (m3).
  • Grit Classifier (1): Grit Classifier TSS (mg/L).
  • COMMON (7): Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), year, month_sin/cos, dow_sin/cos.
  • Row count: Grab ~780-784 train / ~204 test. Composite ~611-633 train / ~193-201 test. Slightly higher than SE1b since inlet NaN rows are no longer binding.
Hypothesis: Isolates whether primary treatment features carry any predictive signal WITHOUT inlet concentrations. Directly contrasts with SE1b (Primary + Inlet + COMMON, 17 features) to determine whether inlet adds or destroys signal at the primary stage. If SE1a and SE1b both produce all-negative R², primary features are genuinely non-predictive regardless of inlet pairing. VIF warning remains: Primary Clarifier pH is near-collinear with itself across years.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

▶ SE1b - Primary + Inlet + COMMON (17 features)
Feature Set
Primary Stage + Inlet + COMMON (17 features)
Columns:
  • Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS - Grab or Composite.
  • Primary Clarifier (5): Primary Clarifier pH, Primary TSS, Primary BOD, Primary COD, Primary Sludge Totalizer (m3).
  • Grit Classifier (1): Grit Classifier TSS (mg/L).
  • COMMON (7): Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), year, month_sin/cos, dow_sin/cos.
  • Row count: Grab ~778 train / 204 test. Composite ~608 train / 181 test (~34% row cost vs Exp1-SE2 baseline; driven by Primary BOD/Grit missingness).
Hypothesis: Tests whether primary clarification and grit removal measurements add predictive value on top of inlet concentrations. Primary stage sits between inlet and secondary in the treatment process. Pearson correlations to effluent targets are weak (|r| < 0.22 for BOD/COD/TSS), though MI scores suggest modest non-linear signal (0.07-0.19). VIF warning: Primary Clarifier pH shows VIF ~2743 in OLS (collinear with inlet pH).
▶ Dataset Details (n_train / n_test / n_features)

▶ SE2 - Secondary Stages

Four scopes tested within SE2, progressing from sub-group isolation to full combination.
• Clarifier + COMMON and Sed + COMMON: isolate each secondary sub-group to test whether they carry distinct or interchangeable signal.
• SE2a - All Secondary only (10 feat, no COMMON): tests the full secondary feature set without operational context, establishing what signal the secondary measurements carry independently. Gains ~50 grab and ~150 comp rows vs SE2-Comb because Flow/Power NaN rows are no longer binding.
• All Secondary + COMMON: full secondary with operational context (17 feat).

▶ Sec Clarifier + COMMON (12 features)
Feature Set
Secondary Clarifier + COMMON (12 features)
Columns:
  • Sec Clarifier (5): pH, TSS, BOD, COD, RAS.
  • COMMON (7): Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), year, month_sin/cos, dow_sin/cos.
  • Removed vs SE1-Combined: Sec Sedimentation group (5 cols) - isolated to test standalone Clarifier signal.
Hypothesis: Isolate the Clarifier group to test whether Clarifier and Sedimentation features carry distinct signal or are interchangeable.

▶ SE2a - All Secondary only (10 features, no COMMON)
Feature Set
All Secondary only (10 features, no COMMON)
Columns:
  • Sec Clarifier (5): pH, TSS (mg/L), BOD (mg/L), COD (mg/L), RAS.
  • Sec Sedimentation (5): pH, TSS (mg/L), BOD (mg/L), COD (mg/L), RAS (New).
  • COMMON: excluded entirely.
  • Row count: Grab ~971-975 train / ~218-220 test. Composite ~785-813 train / ~206-215 test. Notably higher than SE2-Comb (+50 grab, +150 comp) because Flow/Power NaN rows are no longer binding.
Hypothesis: Isolates secondary-stage signal from plant-level operational context (Flow, Power, calendar). Motivated by the Exp1 finding that COMMON sometimes hurts grab targets and substantially helps composite targets. Contrasts with SE2-Comb (All Secondary + COMMON, 17 features) to attribute how much of the SE2 signal comes from secondary measurements vs COMMON features. The extra ~50 grab / ~150 comp rows (vs SE2-Comb) are a secondary benefit of dropping COMMON.

▶ All Secondary + COMMON (17 features)
Feature Set
Combined Secondary + COMMON (15 features)
Columns:
  • Sec Clarifier (5): pH, TSS, BOD, COD, RAS.
  • Sec Sedimentation (5): pH, TSS, BOD, COD, RAS (New).
  • COMMON (5): Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), year, month_sin/cos, dow_sin/cos.
  • Excluded: No inlet features - secondary-only scope.
Hypothesis: Tests secondary treatment process data only (no inlet). Do downstream process indicators predict effluent better than inlet?

▶ SE3 - Inlet + Primary + Secondary + COMMON (27 features)

Full stage-based combination: core inlet (4), primary clarifier + grit (6), secondary clarifier + sedimentation (10), and COMMON (7) = 27 features. Grab train ~764 rows / test 197; Composite train ~595 / test 176. Secondary co-occurs almost perfectly with Primary days, so adding it costs only ~14 rows versus SE1 (Primary + Inlet, ~778 train). The CIS baseline (Core Inlet + Secondary + Common, 21 features, ~920 train) is the prior Exp2-SE2 result; compare SE3 against it to assess whether primary data adds value in the presence of secondary measurements. The Secondary Only - Feature Selected variant (first below) tests whether pruning the pure secondary pool to its most informative features matches or beats the richer CIS feature set.

▶ Secondary Only - Feature Selected (10 feat -> selected)
Feature Set
Secondary Only - Feature Selected (10 feat -> selected)
Columns:
  • Starting set: Same 10 secondary features as SE2a (no COMMON, no Inlet).
  • Linear FS: LassoCV pre-screening for OLS (1-4 features retained per target). Ridge and ElNet select implicitly via regularisation.
  • Non-linear FS: OOF permutation importance (threshold 5%); 2-6 features retained. Three-phase protocol: full CV search -> OOF selection -> refit on selected.
  • Row count: Same as SE2a (Grab ~971-975 / ~785-813 Composite). No additional row cost from FS.
  • Consistent finding: Sec Clarifier BOD and Sec Sed COD are the two features retained most frequently across all models and targets.
Hypothesis: Tests whether feature selection on the pure secondary feature pool improves generalisation over the full 10-feature set (SE2a). With only 10 starting features the marginal benefit of FS is expected to be small; the key question is whether it outperforms CIS-FS (21 features -> selected), which includes inlet and COMMON context. A better Secondary Only-FS result would confirm that COMMON and inlet add no net value once noise features are pruned from the secondary pool.
▶ Dataset Details - Per-Model (n_train / n_test / n_features selected)

Model-specific feature selection: each model may select a different number of features. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen; ElNet selects via internal L1; RF/GB/XGB use OOF permutation importance. n_features shown = selected count per model.

▶ Full Feature Set (27 features)
Feature Set
Inlet + Primary + Secondary + COMMON (27 features)
Columns:
  • Core Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS - Grab or Composite.
  • Primary Clarifier (5): Primary Clarifier pH, Primary TSS, Primary BOD, Primary COD, Primary Sludge Totalizer (m3).
  • Grit Classifier (1): Grit Classifier TSS (mg/L).
  • Sec Clarifier (5): pH, TSS, BOD, COD, RAS.
  • Sec Sedimentation (5): pH, TSS, BOD, COD, RAS (New).
  • COMMON (7): Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), year, month_sin/cos, dow_sin/cos.
  • Row count: Grab ~764 train / 197 test. Composite ~595 train / 176 test. Secondary co-occurs almost perfectly with Primary days (~14-row additional cost vs SE1).
Hypothesis: Full stage-based feature set combining all three monitoring points. Directly tests whether primary clarifier data adds predictive value on top of the CIS baseline (Core Inlet + Secondary + Common, ~920 train rows). SE1 showed primary aloneadds no signal; this experiment tests whether primary is informative in the presence of secondary data.

▶ Core Inlet + Secondary + Common (CIS) - 21 features
Feature Set
Core Inlet + Secondary + Common (CIS) - 21 features
Columns:
  • Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS - Grab or Composite.
  • Secondary (10): Sec Clarifier (5) + Sec Sedimentation (5).
  • COMMON (7): Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW), year, month_sin/cos, dow_sin/cos (cyclic encoding).
Hypothesis: Full combined feature set with corrected calendar encoding. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screening; trees use OOF permutation importance feature selection, both stored in the same results file.

▶ CIS - Feature Selected
Feature Set
CIS - Feature Selected
Columns:
  • Starting set: SE2 combined features (19: Inlet + Secondary + COMMON).
  • Feature selection: OLS uses LassoCV; trees use OOF permutation importance (threshold 5%).
  • Remaining: subset of most predictive combined features per model/target.
Hypothesis: Remove redundant or low-signal features from the full Exp2-S2 set.
▶ Dataset Details - Per-Model (n_train / n_test / n_features selected)

Model-specific feature selection: each model may select a different number of features. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen; ElNet selects via internal L1; RF/GB/XGB use OOF permutation importance. n_features shown = selected count per model.

Test R²: ≥0.6 strong · 0.4-0.6 good · 0.2-0.4 moderate · 0-0.2 weak · <0 fails baseline. Best per row: ★ highest Test R².

R² Gap (Train − Test): ■ <0.10 OK · ■ 0.10-0.25 mild overfit · ■ >0.25 severe overfit - treat result with caution.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

Inline deltas are shown for feature-selected variants only. For Test R², positive Δ means the FS model improved. For Gap, RMSE, MAE, and MdAE, positive Δ means the metric decreased versus the full-feature baseline.

How to read. Green cells = best among the three candidates (SE2-Only, SE2-Comb, SE3). Win (raw): of the 48 model x target pairs, how many does this SE win on raw Test R²? Mean GAJ-R² is the primary tiebreaker: it equals R²_test minus 50% of any gap above zero, so it penalises overfitting even when raw R² looks similar. If Mean R² and Win (raw) are close between two candidates, use Mean GAJ-R² to pick the less overfit one.

▶ Findings - Experiment 2
Q1 Do primary-stage features generalise, and does adding inlet rescue them?

Primary-stage features do not generalise in either scope. SE1a (Primary + COMMON) averages -0.879 with 3/48 positive cells; SE1b (Primary + Inlet + COMMON) averages -0.942 with 3/48 positive cells. Adding core inlet to primary changes mean Test R² by -0.064 (25/48 improved, 18/48 regressed). The failure is therefore not just missing inlet context: primary measurements are weak predictors for this 2025 holdout.

Q2 What does the secondary-only scope add, and does COMMON repay its row cost?

Secondary measurements are the first useful process signal. SE1b -> SE2-Only improves mean Test R² by +1.054 (47/48 improved, 1/48 regressed), and SE2-Only itself averages +0.111 with 36/48 positive cells.
Adding COMMON to all secondary features (SE2-Only -> SE2-Comb) changes mean Test R² by -0.169; Grab -0.087, Composite -0.250. The COMMON columns cost rows in this section (SE2-Only avg train n=889; SE2-Comb avg train n=841) and do not consistently repay that cost. Secondary-only feature selection is also mixed: SE2-Only -> Sec-Only-FS = -0.090 (12/48 improved, 18/48 regressed).

Q3 How do Clarifier, Sedimentation, and All Secondary compare?

Sedimentation is clearly stronger than Clarifier, but combining both is not a clean win. Clarifier averages -0.445; Sedimentation averages +0.034; Sed - Clarifier = +0.479.
Clarifier -> SE2-Comb = +0.388 (46/48 improved, 2/48 regressed), while Sedimentation -> SE2-Comb = -0.091 (25/48 improved, 22/48 regressed). This says the combined pool adds some Clarifier-side cases over the weak Clarifier baseline, but it can dilute the cleaner Sedimentation-only signal.

Q4 Which current Exp2 comparison scope wins, and what is the strategic takeaway?

The current comparison table does not crown the largest feature set. Best mean Test R² is SE2-Only (+0.111); best mean gap-adjusted R² is CIS-FS (-0.178). Raw win count is led by SE2-Only (14/48 cells), while gap-adjusted wins are led by Sec-Only-FS (13/48 cells).
CIS -> SE3 changes mean Test R² by +0.045; CIS-FS -> SE3 changes it by -0.129. Strategically, Exp2 says to keep secondary process data and use inlet+secondary references as controls, but do not assume primary-stage features or the 27-feature all-stage set improve generalisation.

Best Model in Experiment 2 avg best Test R² = 0.444 · Grab avg 0.520 · Composite avg 0.367

raw: ElNet +0.600 (Exp2 CIS - Core Inlet + Secondary + COMMON)
Grab pHElNet+0.308+0.250Exp2 SE2-Comb - All Secondary + COMMON

⚠ Overfitting note: Comp BOD, Comp COD show |gap| > 0.25 even on the gap-adj winner - treat these results with caution.

Selection: winner chosen by gap-adjusted score = R² − 0.5 · max(0, |gap| − 0.10), consistent with the global leaderboard methodology. Where the raw R² winner differs from the gap-adj winner it is shown in orange below the source label. avg best Test R² uses the raw per-target best for cross-experiment comparability.

Experiment 3 - Feature Audit Tiers & All Features

Experiment 3 starts from the Exp2-SE2 baseline (Inlet + Secondary + COMMON, 21 features) and adds features in four steps guided by the EDA Feature Audit.

SE1 (ADD-tier, 28 features) adds the seven aeration-basin measurements that scored MI ≥ 0.20 with marginal row cost ≤ 20 %: Aeration DO/MLSS/SV30/SVI (Existing), Aeration pH (Existing), Aeration DO/SV30 (New). Grab training rows stay near ~816 - no meaningful row loss. No feature selection applied.

SE2 (ADD + CONSIDER-tier, 31 features, full and FS) adds three CONSIDER-tier features (Aeration SVI New, Inlet Total Coliform, Primary Sludge Totalizer). Inlet Total Coliform alone causes ~347 row loss (816 → 469 Grab). Feature selection (OLS: LassoCV; trees: OOF permutation importance) is applied inside training scripts to identify the most predictive subset.

SE3 (ADD + CONSIDER minus Coliform, 30 features, full and FS) removes Inlet Total Coliform from SE2, recovering ~347 rows (469 → 815 Grab). This isolates the row-cost question: is SE2's performance driven by the Coliform signal or by data starvation from Coliform missingness? Both no-FS and FS variants evaluated.

SE4 (All remaining features, 44/39, full and FS) includes every non-leakage column - the all-features upper bound. Joint dropna reduces Grab training to ~130 rows (74% loss vs Exp2-SE2) driven by TKN, Grit Classifier TSS, Primary COD/TSS. Both no-FS and FS variants confirm that marginal row cost is the binding constraint.

▶ SE1 - ADD-tier: Inlet + Secondary + Aeration (no FS)
Feature Set
Exp2-S2 + ADD-tier Aeration Features (28 features, no FS)
Columns:
  • Baseline (21): Exp2-SE2 - Inlet (4) + Secondary (10) + COMMON (7: Flow, Power, year, month/dow cyclic).
  • Added - ADD-tier (7): Aeration DO/MLSS/SV30/SVI/pH (Existing) + Aeration DO/SV30 (New). MI >= 0.20, marginal row cost <= 20%.
  • Row count: Grab ~816 train / 202 test. Composite ~632 train / 181 test. Near-zero row loss vs Exp2-SE2.
Hypothesis: Expand with aeration basin data that shows high mutual information and low missingness cost on top of the Exp2-SE2 baseline. No feature selection applied - tests the full ADD-tier set.

▶ SE2 - ADD + CONSIDER-tier (full + FS)
▶ Full Feature Set (32 features, no FS)
Feature Set
Exp2-S2 + ADD + CONSIDER-tier Features (31 features, no FS)
Columns:
  • SE1 base (28): Inlet + Secondary + COMMON + ADD-tier aeration (7).
  • Added - CONSIDER-tier (3): Aeration SVI (New), Inlet Total Coliform (CFU/100ml), Primary Sludge Totalizer (m3).
  • Removed vs original SE2: Power GE (KW) - redundant: Power Total = Power GE + Power NEA.
  • Row cost: Grab ~469 train / 168 test (Coliform causes ~347-row loss vs SE1). Composite ~423 train / 152 test.
Hypothesis: Baseline (no FS) for the ADD+CONSIDER-tier feature set. Tests the raw performance of 32 features without model-specific selection, enabling a direct comparison with the FS variant (Exp3-S2-FS).

▶ With Feature Selection
Feature Set
Exp2-S2 + ADD + CONSIDER-tier Features with FS (31 features -> 5-24 selected)
Columns:
  • Starting set: Same 31 features as SE2 (SE1 + CONSIDER-tier, Power GE excluded).
  • Feature selection: OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen. RF/GB/XGB use 3-phase OOF permutation importance (threshold=0.05).
  • Ridge/ElNet: Full 31-feature set - L2/L1+L2 regularisation handles collinearity internally.
  • Row count: Same as SE2 (~469 Grab / ~423 Composite train). No dataset rebuild.
Hypothesis: Tests whether CONSIDER-tier features (higher missingness cost) add value when combined with model-specific feature selection. Key trade-off: CONSIDER features increase signal but reduce training rows significantly.
▶ Dataset Details - Per-Model (n_train / n_test / n_features selected)

Model-specific feature selection: each model may select a different number of features. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen; ElNet selects via internal L1; RF/GB/XGB use OOF permutation importance. n_features shown = selected count per model.

▶ SE3 - ADD + CONSIDER minus Coliform (full + FS)
▶ Full Feature Set (no FS)
Feature Set
ADD + CONSIDER minus Inlet Total Coliform (30 features, no FS)
Columns:
  • Removed from SE2: Inlet Total Coliform (CFU/100ml, Grab) - the single highest-missingness CONSIDER-tier feature; caused ~347 row loss (816→469 Grab) in SE2.
  • Remaining (30 features): Inlet (4 Grab or Composite), Sec Clarifier (5) + Sec Sedimentation (5), Flow, Power Total, year, month/dow cyclic (4), ADD-tier aeration (7: DO/MLSS/SV30/SVI/pH Existing + DO/SV30 New), CONSIDER-tier remainder (2: Aeration SVI New, Primary Sludge Totalizer).
  • Row recovery: Grab train ~815 rows (vs ~469 in SE2), Composite train ~631 rows (vs ~423 in SE2). Nearly SE1-level data volume.
Hypothesis: Tests whether the row loss from Inlet Total Coliform was the primary driver of SE2's inconsistent performance. Removing just that one feature recovers ~347 Grab rows at the cost of one CONSIDER-tier signal. Paired with SE3-FS to test whether feature selection further improves results at the higher row count.

▶ With Feature Selection
Feature Set
ADD + CONSIDER minus Inlet Total Coliform with Model-Specific FS (30 features -> selected)
Columns:
  • Starting set: Same 30 features as SE3 (SE2 minus Inlet Total Coliform).
  • Feature selection: OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen (TimeSeriesSplit). RF/GB/XGB use 3-phase OOF permutation importance (threshold=0.05).
  • Ridge/ElNet: Full 30-feature set - L2/L1+L2 regularisation handles collinearity internally.
  • Row count: Same as SE3 (~815 Grab / ~631 Composite train). No dataset rebuild needed (no high-missingness columns in starting set).
Hypothesis: With ~815 Grab training rows (vs ~469 in SE2-FS), OOF folds contain ~272 rows instead of ~156 - permutation importance estimates should be more stable. Tests whether FS on the larger SE3 dataset outperforms SE2-FS.
▶ Dataset Details - Per-Model (n_train / n_test / n_features selected)

Model-specific feature selection: each model may select a different number of features. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen; ElNet selects via internal L1; RF/GB/XGB use OOF permutation importance. n_features shown = selected count per model.

Test R²: ≥0.6 strong · 0.4-0.6 good · 0.2-0.4 moderate · 0-0.2 weak · <0 fails baseline. Best per row: ★ highest Test R².

R² Gap (Train − Test): ■ <0.10 OK · ■ 0.10-0.25 mild overfit · ■ >0.25 severe overfit - treat result with caution.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

Inline deltas are shown for feature-selected variants only. For Test R², positive Δ means the FS model improved. For Gap, RMSE, MAE, and MdAE, positive Δ means the metric decreased versus the full-feature baseline.

▶ SE4 - All Features (full + FS)
▶ All Features (no FS)
Feature Set
All Available Features - no FS (44 Grab / 39 Composite features)
Columns:
  • Added beyond SE2: TKN, NH3-N, Fecal/Total Coliform, Grit Classifier TSS, Primary COD/TSS, Power GE/NEA, Primary Sludge Totalizer, and all remaining measured columns not excluded for leakage or redundancy.
  • Remaining (44 Grab / 39 Composite features): Every non-leakage, non-redundant column from All_Years_Full.xlsx.
  • Row cost: Grab train ~130 rows (74% loss vs Exp2-SE2 baseline), Composite train ~498 rows. Joint dropna from high-missingness columns (TKN, Grit Classifier TSS) drives the collapse.
Hypothesis: Establishes the ceiling of feature availability and tests whether adding all available measurements, despite severe row loss, improves prediction. No feature selection applied - exposes raw overfitting risk at n/p ~= 3. Confirms the Feature Audit methodology: marginal row cost is the binding constraint.

▶ With Feature Selection
Feature Set
All Available Features with Model-Specific FS (44/39 features -> 1-30 selected)
Columns:
  • Starting set: Same 44/39 all features as SE4 full.
  • Feature selection: OLS uses LassoCV. Trees use OOF permutation importance (threshold=0.05). OOF fold size at n=130 with 3-fold TSS is ~43 rows - noisy estimates.
  • Dataset rebuild: OLS and tree models rebuild the dataset from All_Years_Full.xlsx after FS, keeping only selected features. Grab n_train can expand from 130 to 216-1133 rows depending on selected features. n_train_ks=130 stored for reference.
Hypothesis: Tests whether data-driven FS can rescue the undersized all-features dataset. The dataset rebuild partially mitigates the row-loss problem. Key finding: OOF noise at n=130 makes FS unstable; rebuild recovers rows but upstream instability limits gains vs SE3-FS (which starts with ~815 rows).
▶ Dataset Details - Per-Model (n_train / n_test / n_features selected)

Model-specific feature selection: each model may select a different number of features. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen; ElNet selects via internal L1; RF/GB/XGB use OOF permutation importance. n_features shown = selected count per model.

Test R²: ≥0.6 strong · 0.4-0.6 good · 0.2-0.4 moderate · 0-0.2 weak · <0 fails baseline. Best per row: ★ highest Test R².

R² Gap (Train − Test): ■ <0.10 OK · ■ 0.10-0.25 mild overfit · ■ >0.25 severe overfit - treat result with caution.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

Inline deltas are shown for feature-selected variants only. For Test R², positive Δ means the FS model improved. For Gap, RMSE, MAE, and MdAE, positive Δ means the metric decreased versus the full-feature baseline.

Transition Summary

Transition Net Mean ΔR²
raw		 Improvements
Δ > +0.01		 Regressions
Δ < −0.01		 Negligible
|Δ| ≤ 0.01		 Gap-Adj Net ΔR²
R²−0.5·max(0,|gap|−0.10)		 Gap-Adj − Raw
overfitting shift		 Verdict
SE1 (ADD) → SE2 (ADD+CONSIDER, no FS)+0.004827/48 (avg +0.301)19/48 (avg -0.416)2/48+0.0460+0.0411Negligible
Raw gains understated - overfitting decreased
SE2 (no FS) → SE2-FS-0.098019/48 (avg +0.378)15/48 (avg -0.794)14/48-0.1203-0.0223Net regression
Raw gains partially inflated by overfitting
SE1 (ADD) → SE2-FS (ADD+CONSIDER)-0.093129/48 (avg +0.404)18/48 (avg -0.900)1/48-0.0743+0.0188Net regression
Overfitting largely unchanged
SE3 (minus Coliform, no FS) → SE3-FS-0.154014/48 (avg +0.212)17/48 (avg -0.609)17/48-0.1970-0.0431Net regression
Raw gains partially inflated by overfitting
SE4 (All Features, no FS) → SE4-FS+0.057417/48 (avg +0.883)14/48 (avg -0.876)17/48+0.1507+0.0933Net improvement
Raw gains understated - overfitting decreased

Reading the table. Net Mean ΔR² is the signed average across all 48 (model × target) cells. SE1 → SE2: adds CONSIDER-tier features (32 total, no FS) on ~469 Grab rows. SE2 → SE2-FS: applies model-specific FS to the same 32-feature set. SE1 → SE2-FS: net effect of both CONSIDER features and FS together. SE3 → SE3-FS: applies FS to the 30-feature set at ~815 Grab rows (Coliform removed). SE4 → SE4-FS: applies FS to the 44/39-feature all-features set on ~130 Grab rows. Gap-Adj − Raw: negative = raw gains inflated by overfitting; positive = gains understated because overfitting decreased. ★ = best raw Test R² per target · ✦ = best gap-adjusted per target (shown only when raw winner has |gap| > 0.10).

Feature Selection Impact - SE2-FS: pre-FS vs post-FS per model

The metrics in the SE2-FS sub-experiment section show post-FS results only. This table shows what each model achieves on the full 31-feature input set (pre-FS) versus the feature-selected subset (post-FS), directly answering whether FS helped or hurt for each model and target. For Ridge and ElNet there is no distinct pre-FS run.

Reading this table. OLS: pre-FS trains on all 31 input features; post-FS refits on the LassoCV-selected subset. RF / GB / XGB: pre-FS trains on all 31 features; post-FS retrains on the OOF-permutation-importance-selected subset. RMSE not stored for pre-FS tree runs (shown for post-FS only). Ridge: no explicit FS - L2 regularisation handles collinearity; the single column is the full-set result. ElNet: L1+L2 selection is internal - n Selected shows non-zero coefficients; no pre-FS run. Both pre-FS and post-FS use the same ~469 Grab / ~423 Composite training rows (no dataset rebuild in SE2). Green DeltaR2 = FS improved generalisation. Red DeltaR2 = FS hurt generalisation (common for tree models when OOF folds are small). A negative pre-FS gap means test R2 exceeded train R2 (underfitting at full feature count).

Model FS Method n Input n Selected Pre-FS
R2 - Gap - RMSE		 Post-FS
R2 - Gap - RMSE		 Delta R2 / Delta Gap

▶ Key Findings - Experiment 3
Q1 How did each SE's feature set affect sample size and performance?

SE1 (ADD-tier, 28 features) retains ~816 Grab training rows - negligible row loss vs Exp2-SE2. Best ADD Grab avg = +0.451; Comp avg = +0.099. RF avg Grab gap = +0.498 - moderate overfitting with 28 features at n=816.

SE2 (ADD+CONSIDER, 31 features): Inlet Total Coliform alone cuts Grab rows from ~816 to ~469 (-43%). With FS, best SE2-FS Grab avg = +0.488, Comp avg = +0.295.

SE3 (ADD+CONSIDER minus Coliform, 30 features): Removing Coliform recovers ~347 rows, restoring Grab to ~815. RF avg Grab gap = +0.457. Best SE3 Grab avg = +0.454, Comp avg = +0.096.

SE4 (All Features, 44/39 features): Grab rows collapsed to ~130 (-74% vs Exp2-SE2). With n/p ≈ 3, OLS avg gap = +2.527, RF avg gap = +0.643. All-features Grab avg = +0.324, Comp avg = +0.149.

Q2 Did SE3 (minus Coliform) isolate the row-cost problem? Did FS variants improve on full sets?

SE3 vs SE2-FS: SE3 removes only Inlet Total Coliform, recovering ~347 rows. The key question is whether the Coliform signal was worth the row cost. SE3 (no FS) at ~815 rows can be directly compared to SE1 (28 features, ~816 rows) and SE2-FS (31 features, ~469 rows).

SE2-FS (ADD+CONSIDER with FS): Mixed results. LassoCV benefits OLS; tree OOF fold size at n=469 is ~156 rows - workable but constrained.

SE4-FS (All Features FS): Did not rescue the grab dataset. OOF fold size on 130 rows (3-fold TSS) is ~43 rows - permutation importance too noisy, causing overly aggressive pruning (2-7 features). Composite targets (n≈498) fared better.

Q3 What is the strategic takeaway for feature engineering?

The Feature Audit tiers (ADD / CONSIDER) exist to avoid the all-features trap. SE3 isolates the Coliform row-cost question: by removing only Coliform, it tests whether SE2's performance was driven by the Coliform signal or by data starvation. SE1 remains the recommended baseline (MI ≥ 0.20, row cost ≤ 20%, ~816 rows).

SE4 confirms the audit's core conclusion: data-driven FS cannot rescue an undersized dataset - the FS algorithm operates in a high-variance regime when fold sizes fall below ~50 rows. Feature selection should be guided by MI signal and row cost before the experiment, not solely by in-script FS at train time.

Best Model in Experiment 3 avg best Test R² = 0.444 · Grab avg 0.557 · Composite avg 0.331
TargetModelTest R²GapSource
Grab BODOLS0.6170.003Exp3 SE2 - ADD+CONSIDER (FS)
Grab CODGB0.5160.170Exp3 SE4-FS - All Features
Grab TSSOLS0.6120.116Exp3 SE2 - ADD+CONSIDER (no FS)
Grab pHRF0.484 ⚠ overfit0.282Exp3 SE4 - All Features
Comp BODRidge0.4610.076Exp3 SE2 - ADD+CONSIDER (FS)
Comp CODGB0.075 ⚠ overfit0.736Exp3 SE4-FS - All Features
Comp TSSElNet0.421-0.106Exp3 SE2 - ADD+CONSIDER (FS)
Comp pHElNet0.3650.005Exp3 SE2 - ADD+CONSIDER (FS)

⚠ Overfitting note: Grab pH, Comp COD show R² gap > 0.25. Best is ranked by Test R² among non-overfit models (gap ≤ 0.25) where available. High-gap winners may reflect the model memorising 2021-2024 patterns absent in 2025 - prefer lower-gap alternatives for deployment.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Experiment 4 - Collinearity Pruning

Experiment 4 tests the hypothesis that removing collinear and derived features from Exp3-SE2 improves generalisation. SE1 manually drops three redundant groups (SVI, New aeration, Sec Sed - feature count 12-19). SE2 applies automated iterative VIF pruning (threshold=10) within the Exp4-SE1 feature pool, reducing to 5-10 features per target and gaining +47 to +293 rows by dropping high-missingness correlated features. Overall result: both sub-experiments refuted the pruning hypothesis. SE1 showed performance degraded universally. SE2 confirmed the pattern: Ridge alone partially recovered on some targets; tree models failed catastrophically. See the finding boxes below for per-sub-experiment analysis.

▶ SE1 - Manual Group Removal (SVI, New Aeration, Sec Clarifier)
Feature Set
Exp3-SE2 minus Three Feature Groups (18 features)
Columns:
  • Removed: Aeration SVI (Existing + New) - derived directly from SV30/MLSS ratio; carries zero independent information.
  • Removed: All New aeration tank columns (DO New, SV30 New) - cross-tank r=0.74-0.84 with Existing tanks; Existing-tank features are the primary ADD-tier contributors. Note: Comp pH OLS did select New-tank features in FS runs, so this removal is not lossless for all targets.
  • Removed: Sec Clarifier (5 columns) - correlated r=0.69-0.87 with Sec Sedimentation group. Exp2 split analysis showed Sec Sedimentation was the stronger standalone predictor; Sec Clarifier is removed here to test whether Sec Sed alone suffices.
  • Remaining (18 features): Inlet (4), Sec Sedimentation (5: pH, TSS, BOD, COD, RAS New), Flow, Power Total, year, Existing aeration (DO, MLSS, SV30, pH), Inlet Total Coliform, Primary Sludge Totalizer. Cyclic calendar features (month/dow sin/cos) added on the fly by training scripts.
Hypothesis: Hypothesis: removing correlated and derived feature groups reduces variance inflation and improves generalisation, especially on composite targets where Exp3-SE2 GB/XGB catastrophically overfit. SE1 tests manual removal; SE2 tests automated VIF-based pruning within the SE1 pool.

▶ SE2 - VIF-based Automated Pruning (threshold = 10)

Motivation for VIF pruning. The Exp3-SE2 feature set contains several correlated groups (Sec Clarifier vs Sec Sed; Aeration MLSS vs SVI; pH buffered across all stages). VIF quantifies this for OLS below. VIF matters primarily for OLS - high VIF inflates coefficient standard errors and causes instability. Ridge and ElasticNet handle collinearity via L2/L1 penalties; tree models are entirely unaffected. SE2 tests whether automated iterative VIF pruning (threshold=10) on the SE1 pool improves generalisation by removing within-group collinearity more precisely than manual removal.

▶ VIF Collinearity Analysis - Exp3-SE2 Feature Sets

Variance Inflation Factor computed on training rows (year < 2025) after listwise deletion. VIF > 10 = problematic collinearity for linear models. VIF 5-10 = moderate. The threshold rule of thumb: VIF > 10 indicates that the variance of a coefficient estimate is inflated by a factor of 10 relative to an orthogonal design - OLS/Ridge predictions are less reliable for such features.

Global across all 8 datasets: 40 HIGH (VIF>10) · 76 MODERATE (VIF 5-10). Concentrated in Sec Sed vs Sec Clarifier pairs (adjacent measurements of the same parameter) and Aeration SVI (mathematically derived from SV30/MLSS). ElasticNet handles collinearity via L1 penalty - this explains why ElNet outperforms OLS/Ridge on composite targets. OLS/Ridge coefficients are directly inflated by collinear pairs.

Notes on VIF removal
  • Hub pattern: a feature appearing in many "Collinear With" rows (e.g. SVI) is a hub - dropping it alone resolves multiple HIGH VIFs simultaneously. Multiple HIGH flags ≠ remove all; count the distinct hubs first.
  • Tree models (RF, XGB) are unaffected - collinearity only inflates OLS/Ridge coefficient variance. ElasticNet handles it automatically via L1 shrinkage. VIF-based removal is relevant only before fitting OLS or Ridge.
▶ Grab Bod 5 HIGH · 9 MOD · 16 OK

n_train (complete rows): 470 · n_features: 30 · 5 collinear pairs (|r| ≥ 0.85) · Suggestion priority: domain logic → missingness cost → MI with target → VIF tiebreaker

Feature Set
Exp4-SE1 + Iterative VIF Pruning (threshold=10) - 7-8 features
Columns:
  • Starting pool: Exp4-SE1 feature set per target (18 features).
  • VIF pruning: Iteratively drop highest-VIF feature until all VIF <= 10. Run on training rows only (no 2025 data leakage).
  • Typical drops: pH features (VIF 600-2400), Inlet BOD/COD (VIF 18-34), MLSS (SV30 survives as biomass proxy).
  • Retained (excluded from VIF): month, day_of_week, year - temporal/categorical, not subject to VIF.
  • Remaining: 7-8 features per target.
Hypothesis: Hypothesis: automated, data-driven VIF pruning removes intra-group multicollinearity more precisely than manual feature removal (Exp4-SE1), yielding a well-conditioned feature matrix where Ridge/ElNet regularisation can work effectively and tree model overfitting may reduce.

Targetn_train (2021-2024) n_test (2025)n_features

Test R²: ≥0.6 strong · 0.4-0.6 good · 0.2-0.4 moderate · 0-0.2 weak · <0 fails baseline. Best per row: ★ highest Test R².

R² Gap (Train − Test): ■ <0.10 OK · ■ 0.10-0.25 mild overfit · ■ >0.25 severe overfit - treat result with caution.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

Reading the table. Net Mean ΔR² is the signed average across all 48 (model x target) cells. E3-SE2 → SE1: removes three feature groups (SVI, New aeration, Sec Sed) from Exp3-SE2. SE1 → SE2: applies automated iterative VIF pruning (threshold=10) on the SE1 pool. E3-SE2 → SE2: combined effect of both pruning steps vs the Exp3-SE2 baseline. Gap-Adj - Raw: negative = raw gains inflated by overfitting; positive = gains understated because overfitting decreased. ★ = best raw Test R² per target · ✦ = best gap-adjusted per target (shown only when raw winner has |gap| > 0.10).

▶ Key Findings - Experiment 4
Q1 How did each SE's feature set affect sample size and performance?

Sample size: Row counts barely changed for SE1 (+5-10, <2%) because Sec Sed and Sec Clarifier are collected on the same operational days (co-occurring missingness). Removing Sec Sed does not unlock any additional rows. SE2 (VIF pruning) unlocked meaningful extra rows by eliminating high-missingness pH and Inlet BOD/COD columns: Comp TSS grew from ~448 to ~741 rows (+65%), Comp COD from ~684 to ~866 (+27%). This row gain is the only genuine benefit of VIF pruning in this experiment.

Performance - SE1: E3-SE2 baseline grab avg = +0.515, comp avg = +0.295. After manual group removal: SE1 grab avg = +0.429, comp avg = +0.300. Performance deteriorated on every target and every model.

Performance - SE2: grab avg = +0.396, comp avg = +0.145. VIF pruning further degraded results on most targets. Ridge grab recovered partially (Ridge grab avg ~+0.327), but Comp pH Ridge collapsed to -1.248.

Q2 Did removing feature groups (manual SE1 or automated SE2) improve generalization?

No - both pruning strategies made things worse. SE1 (manual removal): removing correlated groups discarded genuine signal. Key degradation: RF Grab COD dropped from +0.40 (Exp3-SE2) to -0.08 (SE1). ElNet Grab BOD: +0.68 to +0.17. The premise that Sec Clarifier alone suffices was wrong - Exp2's split analysis had already shown Sec Sedimentation was the stronger standalone predictor, and FS results from Exp3-SE2 confirm both groups carry non-redundant signal.

SE2 (VIF pruning): VIF is target-agnostic - it removes features correlated with other features regardless of their signal value. Inlet BOD is highly correlated with other inlet features, but it also directly predicts Effluent BOD. Sec Clarifier pH is collinear with other pH columns, but it carries the strongest pH signal for effluent pH prediction. VIF pruning strips precisely the features that matter most. Tree models collapsed catastrophically when pH features and Inlet BOD/COD were removed.

Q3 What is the strategic takeaway for collinearity handling?

Collinearity in this dataset is best handled by model-side regularisation, not manual feature removal. Ridge and ElasticNet suppress collinear features automatically via L2/L1 penalties. Tree models (RF, GB, XGB) are unaffected by collinearity - correlated features simply share split importance, and removing them reduces split diversity, which worsens overfitting.

The collinearity between Sec Clarifier and Sec Sed, or between aeration Existing and New tanks, reflects real process physics: the plant runs in a correlated steady state. These correlations are not a modelling problem; they are a feature of the data that regularised models handle correctly.

Exp3-SE2 remains the recommended feature set for all models. The only benefit from SE2 (VIF) is the row gain on composite targets, which could be recovered more cleanly by explicitly excluding high-missingness features at dataset-build time rather than through VIF-driven pruning.

Best Model in Experiment 4 avg best Test R² = 0.400 · Grab avg 0.451 · Composite avg 0.350
TargetModelTest R²GapSource
Grab BODRidge0.514-0.014Exp4 SE1
Grab CODOLS0.4700.171Exp4 SE2 (overfit suppressed)
Grab TSSElNet0.4750.085Exp4 SE2
Grab pHOLS0.1000.166Exp4 SE2 (overfit suppressed)
Comp BODRF0.472-0.141Exp4 SE1
Comp CODElNet0.1200.224Exp4 SE2 (overfit suppressed)
Comp TSSRF0.500-0.187Exp4 SE1
Comp pHElNet0.2960.100Exp4 SE1

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Experiment 5 - Cross-Type Inlet Features

Experiment 5 tests the cross-type inlet hypothesis: do grab-sample inlet measurements help predict composite effluent, and vice versa? The base feature set is Exp3-SE1 ADD-tier (28 features, Inlet + Secondary + COMMON + 7 ADD aeration columns, selected for near-zero row cost). Four cross-type inlet columns are added per sub-experiment.

SE1 (Composite targets + Grab inlet, 32 features): Composite prediction datasets receive GRAB_INLET (Inlet pH/BOD/COD/TSS Grab). Row cost: ~22.7% (816 to 629 train rows) - within acceptable range.

SE2 (Grab targets + Composite inlet, 32 features): Grab prediction datasets receive COMP_INLET (Inlet pH/BOD/COD/TSS Composite). Row cost: ~38.2% (1021 to 630 train rows) - high but unavoidable; both grab and composite inlet must be measured on the same day. Retained n (~630) is comparable to Exp2-SE2 grab training size.

The experiment answers: does same-day co-measurement of the complementary inlet sample type add independent predictive signal beyond the same-type inlet columns?

▶ SE1 - Comp + Grab Inlet (full + FS)
▶ Full Feature Set (32 features)
Feature Set
Composite Targets: Composite Inlet + Grab Inlet + Secondary + ADD-tier (32 features)
Columns:
  • Composite Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS (Composite) - native inlet.
  • Added - Grab Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS (Grab) - cross-type signal.
  • Secondary (10): Sec Clarifier (5) + Sec Sedimentation (5).
  • COMMON + ADD-tier (14): Flow, Power, year, month/dow cyclic (7) + ADD-tier aeration (7).
  • Targets: Composite BOD, COD, TSS, pH only. Row cost: ~22.7% vs SE1 base (~816 to ~629 train).
Hypothesis: Tests whether grab inlet concentrations (same-day co-measurement) provide complementary signal for composite effluent prediction. Row cost: ~22.7% (816 → 629 train rows) due to joint grab + composite sampling availability.

▶ With Feature Selection
Feature Set
Exp5-SE1 with Model-Specific Feature Selection (32 features -> selected)
Columns:
  • Starting set: Same 32 features as SE1 (Composite + Grab Inlet + Secondary + ADD-tier).
  • Feature selection: OLS uses LassoCV; trees use OOF permutation importance (threshold=0.05). Composite targets only.
  • Row count: Same as SE1 (~629 Composite train).
Hypothesis: Tests whether model-specific FS can improve generalisation on the 32-feature Exp5-SE1 dataset (composite targets + cross-type grab inlet columns). Paired with Exp5-S1 (full feature set) for direct comparison.
▶ Dataset Details - Per-Model (n_train / n_test / n_features selected)

Model-specific feature selection: each model may select a different number of features. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screen; ElNet selects via internal L1; RF/GB/XGB use OOF permutation importance. n_features shown = selected count per model.

▶ SE2 - Grab + Comp Inlet (full + FS)
▶ Full Feature Set (32 features)
Feature Set
Grab Targets: Grab Inlet + Composite Inlet + Secondary + ADD-tier (32 features)
Columns:
  • Grab Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS (Grab) - native inlet.
  • Added - Composite Inlet (4): pH, BOD, COD, TSS (Composite) - cross-type signal.
  • Secondary (10): Sec Clarifier (5) + Sec Sedimentation (5).
  • COMMON + ADD-tier (14): Flow, Power, year, month/dow cyclic (7) + ADD-tier aeration (7).
  • Targets: Grab BOD, COD, TSS, pH only. Row cost: ~38.2% vs SE1 base (~1021 to ~630 train).
Hypothesis: Tests whether composite inlet concentrations (integrated daily sample) provide complementary signal for grab effluent prediction. Row cost: ~38.2% (1021 → 631 train rows) - unavoidable due to joint grab + composite sampling days.

▶ Key Findings - Experiment 5
Q1 Did adding cross-type inlet features improve prediction for each target type?

SE1 (Composite + Grab inlet): avg composite Test R² = +0.134 vs Exp3-SE1 baseline +0.099. Marginal delta: +0.035. Row cost ~22.7% (629 from 816 train rows).

SE2 (Grab + Composite inlet): avg grab Test R² = +0.426 vs Exp3-SE1 baseline +0.451. Marginal delta: -0.025. Row cost ~38.2% (630 from 1021 train rows).

Compare deltas against the row-cost paid: if the cross-type columns add less than the baseline R² would have gained from 22-38% more training rows, they carry no net value.

Q2 What are the confounds in interpreting the cross-type experiment?

The cross-type inlet hypothesis has an inherent baseline contamination: by requiring co-occurrence of grab and composite inlet measurements, we have changed the training population. Days with both grab and composite measurements may be systematically different from days with only one type (e.g. more routine vs storm-event days).

Interpretation rule: a positive delta confirms cross-type signal, but a negative or zero delta may reflect selection bias (the joint-availability filter) rather than absence of cross-type signal. To isolate the pure feature effect, compare Exp5-SE1/S2 directly against Exp3-SE1 trained on the same restricted rows (an Exp3-SE1 re-run with joint-availability mask). This is a natural follow-up experiment.

Best Model in Experiment 5 avg best Test R² = 0.316 · Grab avg 0.450 · Composite avg 0.182
TargetModelTest R²GapSource
Grab BODRF0.515 ⚠ overfit0.390Exp5 SE2 - Grab + Composite Inlet
Grab CODRF0.4940.208Exp5 SE2-FS - Grab + Composite Inlet (FS) (overfit suppressed)
Grab TSSRF0.476-0.197Exp5 SE2 - Grab + Composite Inlet
Grab pHRF0.264 ⚠ overfit0.535Exp5 SE2 - Grab + Composite Inlet
Comp BODRF0.299 ⚠ overfit0.594Exp5 SE1 - Composite + Grab Inlet
Comp CODRF-0.091 ⚠ overfit0.679Exp5 SE1-FS - Composite + Grab Inlet (FS)
Comp TSSRF0.2580.227Exp5 SE1 - Composite + Grab Inlet
Comp pHElNet0.2600.168Exp5 SE1 - Composite + Grab Inlet

⚠ Overfitting note: Grab BOD, Grab pH, Comp BOD, Comp COD show R² gap > 0.25. Best is ranked by Test R² among non-overfit models (gap ≤ 0.25) where available. High-gap winners may reflect the model memorising 2021-2024 patterns absent in 2025 - prefer lower-gap alternatives for deployment.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Advanced Methods

This section evaluates two classes of advanced model architectures on the Exp3-SE2 feature set (~25 features per target: Inlet + Secondary + ADD+CONSIDER-tier + cyclic calendar), selected as the richest validated set at the time (ElNet Test R²=0.684 Grab BOD, RF 0.504 Grab TSS).

Neural Networks: MLPRegressor (feedforward ANN) failed on all datasets tested (avg R²=-1.12 on Exp3-SE2 features). The data-volume diagnostic (ANN Exploration sub-section) confirmed the failure is not data-volume limited - tripling training rows worsened performance. See the rationale card below for why a feedforward ANN was chosen over RNN/LSTM architectures.

Ensemble Methods: Voting (ElNet+RF+XGB, equal weights) is the recommended approach - avg Test R²=+0.287, Grab BOD 0.692. Stacking (walk-forward OOF, no look-ahead bias after KFold correction) is methodologically clean but weaker (avg R²=+0.098).

Neural Networks

Why a Feedforward ANN (MLP) rather than RNN / LSTM?

Three structural constraints made recurrent architectures a poor fit for this dataset:

  • Gapped time series (~30-40% missing days). LSTM / RNN require a dense sequential input stream. Wastewater measurements - especially composite samples - are only collected on certain operational days, leaving long irregular gaps. Filling these gaps with imputation would introduce substantial synthetic data into a model whose value comes from learning genuine temporal dynamics. A feedforward model treats each observation as an independent row and is unaffected by missingness in neighbouring dates.
  • Largely tabular daily structure. Each day's prediction is well-described by its own inlet concentrations, secondary clarifier readings, and process conditions. Temporal signal that does exist (seasonal cycles, weekday patterns) is compactly encoded by the cyclic calendar features already present (month_sin/cos, dow_sin/cos). Explicit temporal context - lag 1/3/7-day and 7-day rolling means - was tested separately in the Temporal Features section, which is more data-efficient than feeding raw hidden state through an LSTM cell.
  • Insufficient training samples for recurrent architectures. With ~470 Grab / ~290 Composite training rows, even a 2-hidden-layer MLP failed severely (every target selected alpha=1.0 - maximum weight decay). An LSTM cell has 4x more parameters than a basic RNN unit and would overfit far worse. The data-volume diagnostic (ANN Exploration) confirmed that tripling training rows to ~1175 still did not rescue performance.

Future conditions for sequential models: If the dataset grows to 2000+ consecutive daily observations with <10% missingness, LSTM is worth revisiting (use a masking layer for irregular gaps). Alternatively, aggregate to weekly averages (~200 data points, minimal gaps) and use a shallow GRU.

▶ ANN (MLPRegressor) FAILED avg R²=-1.12
Feature Set
Exp3-SE2 Features - ANN (MLPRegressor, StandardScaler pipeline)
Columns:
  • Features: Same as Exp3-SE2 (~25 per target: Inlet + Secondary + COMMON + ADD + CONSIDER subset).
  • Model: StandardScaler + MLPRegressor, GridSearchCV on hidden_layer_sizes and alpha (TimeSeriesSplit).
Hypothesis: Tests whether a neural network can outperform regularised linear and tree models. Key challenge: ~470 training samples may be insufficient for a multi-layer perceptron.

▶ ANN Failure Post-Mortem avg Test R²=-1.123 · avg Gap=+1.865

Grid search: 5 architectures × 4 regularisation values = 20 combinations per target. Fixed: relu, adam, max_iter=2000, early_stopping=True (validation_fraction=0.10, n_iter_no_change=20). Tuning via TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=3). All targets selected alpha=1.0 (maximum regularisation).

6 of 8 targets have Test R² ≤ 0. ANN not recommended at current sample sizes.

Comp COD5151730.3(640.1, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (64,)0.653-0.2810.935FAILED+0.024

n/p = training rows ÷ features. Red = n/p < 20 (underdetermined - high overfitting risk). MLPs typically need n/p ≥ 100-200 for stable generalisation on tabular data. Δ vs Voting = ANN Test R² − Phase 9 Voting Test R².

▶ Root-Cause Analysis

1. Sample-to-feature ratio. Worst case: Comp TSS - 290 training rows, 27 features → n/p = 10.7. MLPs require far more data than tree ensembles to generalise from small tabular datasets. Typical guidance: n/p ≥ 100-200 for stable MLP generalisation.

2. Temporal distribution shift. 2025 test data shows different distributional properties than 2021-2024 training data (Q4-Flow and DJF errors 2-3× higher - see Error Regime Decomposition). MLPs form highly non-linear decision boundaries that diverge further from shifted data than regularised linear models or bagged trees.

3. Early stopping did not prevent 2025 failure. Early stopping (validation_fraction=0.10, n_iter_no_change=20) monitors in-sample generalisation on the 2021-2024 fold, not 2025 generalisation. Gaps range from +0.41 (Grab COD) to +6.77 (Comp pH) despite early stopping - confirming that early stopping alone cannot address distributional shift.

4. Network capacity is not the bottleneck. Best architectures are small - (64,) or (128,) single hidden layer with α=1.0 for 6 of 8 targets. The grid search found that more capacity hurts; adding layers would worsen the gaps. The problem is generalisation, not expressiveness.

Future conditions for ANN. Dataset growth to n ≥ 3,000 per target and an LSTM architecture (explicit temporal modelling via hidden state rather than hand-crafted lags) are the recommended prerequisites before revisiting neural networks on this problem.

ANN Dataset Exploration - Data-Volume Diagnostic

The Phase 9 ANN failed on Exp3-SE2 datasets (avg Test R²=−1.12) due to insufficient training samples (~470 Grab, ~290 Composite). These runs test the same ANN architecture on earlier, data-richer experiment datasets to isolate whether the failure was data-volume limited or reflects a fundamental ANN limitation on this type of data.

Three datasets are tested: Exp1 (Inlet + COMMON, 9 features, ~1175 Grab / ~800 Composite rows - 2.5× more data), Exp2-SE1 (Secondary + COMMON, 15 features, ~924 / ~740 rows), and Exp2-SE2 (Inlet + Secondary + COMMON, 19 features, ~920 / ~733 rows). The hyperparameter grid includes larger architectures (256-128, 128-64-32 hidden layers) compared to Phase 9 ANN, appropriate for the larger sample counts.

Feature Set

  • Model: StandardScaler + MLPRegressor, GridSearchCV on hidden_layer_sizes and alpha (TimeSeriesSplit).
  • Row count: ~1175 Grab / ~800 Composite training rows.
Hypothesis: Phase 9 ANN failed with ~470 Grab training samples. Exp1 has 2.5× more data with simpler features. Tests whether ANN failure was data-volume limited. Same architecture as Phase 9 ANN; only dataset changed.

Ensemble Methods

▶ Voting Ensemble (ElNet + RF + XGBoost) RECOMMENDED
Feature Set
Exp3-SE2 Features - Voting Ensemble (ElNet + RF + XGBoost, equal weights)
Columns:
  • Features: Same as Exp3-SE2 (~25 per target).
  • Ensemble: VotingRegressor averaging ElasticNet, RandomForest, XGBoost with equal weights.
  • Composition: Ridge removed (L2 duplicates ElNet); XGBoost added for structural diversity (boosting vs bagging vs linear).
Hypothesis: Ensemble averaging reduces variance through uncorrelated errors. ElNet: regularised linear with feature selection (L1). RF: bagging over decision trees, robust to outliers. XGB: boosted trees, captures residual non-linearities. Three structurally distinct inductive biases.

▶ Stacking Ensemble (ElNet + RF + XGB - Ridge, walk-forward OOF) CONSISTENT - no leakage
Feature Set
Exp3-SE2 Features - Stacking (ElNet + RF + XGB -> Ridge meta, walk-forward OOF)
Columns:
  • Features: Same as Exp3-SE2 (~25 per target).
  • Base learners: ElNet + RF + XGBoost. Meta-learner: Ridge (tuned alpha).
  • OOF generation: Walk-forward TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5). First ~1/6 of training excluded from meta-learner (no OOF coverage). No look-ahead bias.
Hypothesis: Replaces original KFold(n_splits=5) StackingRegressor which had look-ahead bias: predicting 2021 data using models trained on 2022-2024. Walk-forward OOF ensures the meta-learner learns how base models perform when projecting forward in time. Approx. 83% OOF coverage across all targets.

Transitions reflect pure architecture differences: ANN vs ensemble methods, and Voting (equal weights) vs Stacking (walk-forward OOF meta-learner). ★ = best raw Test R² per target (across all 6 variants) · ✦ = best gap-adjusted per target (shown only when raw winner has |gap| > 0.10).

▶ Negative R² Diagnosis - Variance Collapse vs Genuine Failure

R² = 1 - SS_res/SS_tot. A low-variance 2025 test set (sigma-ratio < 0.5) shrinks the denominator and forces R² negative even when absolute accuracy is unchanged or improving. ΔMAE and ΔRMSE (2025 - 2024) give scale-grounded evidence: negative = model improved in absolute terms; positive = genuine deterioration. NRMSE = RMSE / (max - min) for scale-normalised cross-target comparison.

Model Target Test R² σ_train σ_test σ-ratio MAE 2024 MAE 2025 ΔMAE RMSE 2024 RMSE 2025 ΔRMSE NRMSE Diagnosis

Voting Comp COD -0.562 27.001 20.576 0.76 9.618 21.330 +11.712 11.999 25.714 +13.715 0.260 Genuine deterioration (MAE ×2.2 from 2024→2025)
Stacking Grab TSS -0.049 12.207 10.047 0.82 2.941 5.773 +2.832 3.880 10.292 +6.412 0.242 Genuine deterioration (MAE ×2.0 from 2024→2025)
Stacking Grab pH -0.260 0.283 0.234 0.83 0.163 0.197 +0.035 0.235 0.263 +0.028 0.271 Mixed - σ-ratio=0.83, MAE ×1.2
Best Model in Advanced Methods avg best Test R² = 0.305 · Grab avg 0.417 · Composite avg 0.193

Grab BODVoting0.5370.181Phase 9 - Voting
Grab CODANN0.487 ⚠ overfit0.413Phase 9 - ANN
Grab TSSStacking-0.0490.154Phase 9 - Stacking (overfit suppressed)
Grab pHVoting0.312 ⚠ overfit0.509Phase 9 - Voting
