⚠ Overfitting note: Grab COD, Grab pH, Comp COD, Comp pH show R² gap > 0.25. Best is ranked by Test R² among non-overfit models (gap ≤ 0.25) where available. High-gap winners may reflect the model memorising 2021-2024 patterns absent in 2025 - prefer lower-gap alternatives for deployment.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

▶ Key Findings - Advanced Methods
Q1Why did the ANN fail while the Voting ensemble succeeded?

The MLP must learn all structure - feature interactions, non-linearities, and regularisation paths - from only ~470 Grab / ~290 Composite training rows. Avg Test R² = -1.123. GridSearchCV selected alpha=1.0 (maximum weight decay) for every target, confirming the model is parameter-starved: it can only fit the data when its weights are maximally shrunk toward zero.

Voting bypasses this by combining three pre-tuned learners with complementary inductive biases: ElNet (handles collinearity, L1 feature selection), RF (bagging over decision trees - robust to the TSS outliers up to 1266 mg/L in training), XGBoost (gradient boosting, captures residual non-linearities). Uncorrelated errors partially cancel in the average, giving avg Test R² = +0.222. The data-volume diagnostic confirmed that sample count is not the bottleneck: tripling rows to ~1175 (Exp1 dataset) worsened ANN performance to avg R² = -5.6.

Q2Does Stacking improve on Voting?

Voting (equal-weight average): avg R² = +0.222. Stacking (walk-forward OOF meta-learner): avg R² = +0.097.

The meta-learner in Stacking (Ridge fitted on ~390 OOF rows with 3 base-learner predictions as features) adds complexity without proportional lift over simple averaging. With ~83% OOF coverage, the first ~17% of the training set is excluded from the meta-learner entirely - those rows are never seen at meta-training time. Stacking is methodologically clean (no look-ahead bias after the KFold correction) but not worth the added complexity at current sample sizes. Verdict: Voting is preferred - simpler, no meta-learner overhead, and consistently better.

Q3Which advanced method is recommended and for which targets?

Voting (ElNet+RF+XGB) is recommended. It has the best average R² across the Advanced Methods panel and leads the Grab-side ensemble results, including Grab BOD +0.537. Stacking is still important in the comparison: it is the better Advanced Method on several Composite targets in the current results, including Comp BOD +0.319, Comp COD -0.033, and Comp TSS +0.205.

For Grab targets only, combining Voting with selective feature engineering (Phase 10b - Selective FE) further improves performance (avg Grab R² ~+0.48 vs Voting base +0.28). For Composite targets, the base Exp3-SE2 feature set (no FE) is safer - FE causes severe overfitting at n_train ~290 rows.

Comp COD is not addressable by any architecture tested: 2025 MAE doubled from 2024 (genuine distribution shift). Flag Comp COD predictions as unreliable until more post-2025 data is collected.

Q4Does more training data rescue the ANN? What did the dataset exploration reveal?

Tripling the training rows from ~470 (Exp3-SE2) to ~1175 (Exp1) did not rescue ANN performance. Exp1 ANN avg Test R² = -5.596 - worse than Phase 9 (Exp3-SE2) ANN avg R² = -1.123. More data cannot compensate for missing secondary process signal.

Secondary features are the prerequisite for positive ANN generalisation. Exp2-SE1 ANN (Secondary + COMMON, 15 features, ~924 rows) is the only configuration achieving positive Grab R²: Grab BOD +0.200, Grab TSS +0.273. Adding inlet concentrations to secondary data (Exp2-SE2, 21 features) does not help Composite targets - Comp BOD collapses from -0.11 (Exp2-SE1) to -1.49 (Exp2-SE2) as the additional features increase overfitting on ~733 Composite rows.

Composite targets fail universally for the ANN across all four dataset configurations tested (Exp1, Exp2-SE1, Exp2-SE2, Exp3-SE2). The ANN cannot capture the distributional shift in composite measurements from training to 2025.

Conclusion: ANN failure is feature-set and temporal-structure limited, not data-volume limited. Even with secondary features and adequate data (~924 rows), the ANN substantially underperforms the Voting ensemble. The ANN is not recommended for any target on this dataset.

Feature Engineering

Phase 10 applies feature engineering (log transforms, interaction terms, outlier flags) on top of the Exp3-SE2 feature set. Phase 10 (full) applies this uniformly to all targets. Phase 10b (selective) applies FE only to grab targets after discovering that composite datasets overfit severely with the expanded feature count.

▶ SE1 - Full Feature Engineering (All Targets) COMPOSITE OVERFIT
Feature Set
Exp3-SE2 + Full Feature Engineering - All Targets (37-52 features)
Columns:
  • Base: Exp3-SE2 features per target.
  • Added: log1p transforms of right-skewed columns + pairwise interaction terms (key pairs) + IQR outlier indicator flags.
  • Applied: uniformly to all 8 targets (Grab + Composite).
  • Total features: 37-52 per target.
Hypothesis: Feature engineering to capture non-linear relationships and reduce skewness. Applied uniformly to test if it universally helps. Hypothesis: log transforms help BOD/COD/TSS (right-skewed).

▶ SE2 - Selective Feature Engineering (Grab FE / Composite Base) RECOMMENDED
Feature Set
Selective Feature Engineering - Grab: Full FE / Composite: Base Exp3-SE2
Columns:
  • Grab targets: Full FE applied (log1p + interactions + IQR flags) - 37-52 features each.
  • Composite targets: Base Exp3-SE2 features only (17-27 features) - no engineering to avoid overfitting (n_train ~290).
Hypothesis: Addresses Phase 10 finding: FE caused severe overfitting on composite datasets (n_train ≈ 290). Selective approach preserves FE gains on grab targets while stabilising composites.

▶ Key Findings - Feature Engineering
Q1What happens when feature engineering is applied uniformly to all targets?

Full FE (SE1): Voting avg Grab = +0.483, Comp = -0.178 vs Phase 9 baseline Grab = +0.399, Comp = +0.046. Composite targets were catastrophically affected: Comp TSS ElNet gap +3.80, Ridge gap +3.31. Root cause: ~290 Composite training rows + 50 engineered features = severe overfitting. Full FE is not suitable for Composite targets at current sample sizes.

Q2Does selective FE recover the composite regressions while preserving Grab gains?

Selective FE (SE2): apply log1p+interactions+IQR flags to Grab targets only; leave Composite targets at base Exp3-SE2 features.

Voting avg Grab = +0.483, Comp = +0.239. Grab targets gain substantially vs baseline (+0.399). Comp TSS fully recovered (Voting +0.349 vs Full FE -1.267). Selective FE is the recommended FE configuration.

Q3Which targets benefited most and which remained unresponsive?

New records for Grab TSS: Voting SE2 = +0.642, Ridge SE2 = +0.633. Grab BOD: Voting SE2 = +0.674 (maintains Phase 10 high).

Comp COD remains unresponsive (best R² = -0.051 across all FE variants). Grab pH is the weakest Grab target (Voting SE2 = +0.244) - narrow dynamic range means engineered interaction terms add noise rather than signal.

Best Model in Feature Engineering avg best Test R² = 0.416 · Grab avg 0.495 · Composite avg 0.336
TargetModelTest R²GapSource
Grab BODVoting0.674-0.136Phase 10 - FE (all)
Grab CODVoting0.3740.231Phase 10b - FE (Grab)
Grab TSSVoting0.642-0.083Phase 10 - FE (all)
Grab pHRF0.291 ⚠ overfit0.401Phase 10 - FE (all)
Comp BODVoting0.553-0.118Phase 10b - FE (Grab)
Comp CODRidge-0.008 ⚠ overfit0.621Phase 10 - FE (all)
Comp TSSElNet0.458-0.031Phase 10b - FE (Grab)
Comp pHElNet0.3400.066Phase 10b - FE (Grab)

⚠ Overfitting note: Grab pH, Comp COD show R² gap > 0.25. Best is ranked by Test R² among non-overfit models (gap ≤ 0.25) where available. High-gap winners may reflect the model memorising 2021-2024 patterns absent in 2025 - prefer lower-gap alternatives for deployment.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Temporal Features + log1p Targets

Phase 11 adds temporal lag features (lag 1/3/7-day) and a 7-day rolling mean to inlet, Flow, and Power columns, plus a log1p target transform on BOD/COD/TSS with Duan smearing back-transform. Feature expansion is restricted to inlet+Flow+Power columns to avoid the catastrophic overfitting seen when applying temporal features to all columns (which would produce ~128 features on ~290 composite rows).

Key wins: Grab BOD (Ridge 0.656, gap −0.095) and Grab COD (XGB 0.464, gap +0.057 - new Grab COD high with an honest gap). Key losses: all composite targets deteriorate, especially Comp TSS (Ridge −1.04, gap +1.82). Conclusion: temporal features help Grab targets; composites lack the sample size to benefit. CV_R² and Gap_gen = CV_R² − Test_R² are recorded per model for distribution-shift diagnosis.

▶ SE1 - Temporal Lags + log1p (All Models)
Feature Set
Exp3-SE2 + Temporal Lags + log1p Target Transform (50-58 features)
Columns:
  • Base: Exp3-SE2 features per target.
  • Added temporal: Lag 1/3/7-day + 7-day calendar rolling mean on inlet + Flow + Power columns only (~50-58 total features).
  • Target transform: log1p on BOD/COD/TSS; pH untransformed. Back-transform via Duan smearing.
  • Restriction: Temporal features limited to inlet+Flow+Power to avoid catastrophic overfitting on Composite (n~290).
Hypothesis: Daily wastewater quality has temporal autocorrelation - yesterday's influent predicts today's effluent better than cross-sectional features alone. Lag expansion restricted to inlet+Flow+Power columns to avoid catastrophic overfitting on composite datasets (n≈290 rows). log1p targets reduce right-skew in BOD/COD/TSS distributions.

▶ Key Findings - Temporal Features
Q1Do temporal lag features help Grab and Composite targets equally?

Grab targets benefited: Grab avg R² = +0.484 vs Phase 10b baseline +0.495. Grab BOD Ridge = +0.656 (gap -0.095 - honest generalisation). Grab COD XGB = +0.464 - new Grab COD high with honest gap (+0.057). Lag features add genuine signal: yesterday's inlet BOD correlates with today's effluent BOD through retention time dynamics.

Composite targets deteriorated: Comp avg R² = +0.227 vs Phase 10b +0.325. Comp TSS Ridge = -1.04 (gap +1.82) - catastrophic. With ~290 Composite training rows and 50-58 features after lag expansion, overfitting is severe.

Q2Why was feature expansion restricted to inlet/Flow/Power columns only?

Lag and rolling features were restricted to inlet + Flow + Power columns only (not every continuous feature). Even with this restriction, 50-58 features result from the expansion. Full expansion across all process columns would produce ~128 features on ~290 composite rows - the ratio is far too high.

This confirms that temporal features are only beneficial when the training-set size is large relative to the added feature count. For composite targets, temporal structure must be captured via the process parameters already present (secondary clarifier/sedimentation data), not via lag engineering.

Q3Where does Phase 11 rank per-target, and what is the overall recommendation?

Per-target winners (from Overfit-Aware Selection):
Grab BOD: Phase 9 Voting (0.692, gap ~0) - Phase 11 Ridge (0.656) is close but lower.
Grab COD: Exp2-SE1 RF (0.489, gap -0.004) - Phase 11 XGB (0.464) is second.
Grab TSS: Phase 10b Voting (0.642). Grab pH: Phase 11 ElNet (0.376).
Comp BOD: Phase 10b Voting (+0.553). Comp COD: no model generalises. Comp TSS: Phase 10b ElNet (0.458). Comp pH: Exp3-SE1 Ridge (0.363).

Temporal features contribute one clear Grab target win (Grab pH) and near-ties on Grab BOD and COD after gap-adjustment. Phase 10b Selective FE remains the overall recommended configuration.

Best Model in Temporal Features avg best Test R² = 0.355 · Grab avg 0.484 · Composite avg 0.227
TargetModelTest R²GapSource
Grab BODRidge0.656-0.095Phase 11 - Temporal
Grab CODXGB0.4640.057Phase 11 - Temporal
Grab TSSRidge0.4400.199Phase 11 - Temporal
Grab pHElNet0.3760.217Phase 11 - Temporal
Comp BODElNet0.332 ⚠ overfit0.293Phase 11 - Temporal
Comp CODElNet0.108 ⚠ overfit0.367Phase 11 - Temporal
Comp TSSXGB0.231 ⚠ overfit0.693Phase 11 - Temporal
Comp pHRidge0.1450.233Phase 11 - Temporal (overfit suppressed)

⚠ Overfitting note: Comp BOD, Comp COD, Comp TSS show R² gap > 0.25. Best is ranked by Test R² among non-overfit models (gap ≤ 0.25) where available. High-gap winners may reflect the model memorising 2021-2024 patterns absent in 2025 - prefer lower-gap alternatives for deployment.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Overfit-Aware Model Selection

Three complementary rules re-rank every (target × experiment) row beyond naive max(Test R²): gap-adjusted score penalises overfit beyond a 10-pt tolerance; the one-SE rule picks the smallest-gap model within 0.03 R² of the top; the Pareto frontier lists all models not dominated on (R²_test ↑, |gap| ↓). RMSE and MAE are shown alongside R² for operational interpretability - they rank identically to R² within a target but carry real units (mg/L or pH) that relate to discharge consent limits.

Why re-rank?

On this dataset (n_test ≈ 160-280 for the held-out 2025 year), Test R² alone is noisy. A model with R²=0.43 and gap=0.88 is not equivalent to one with R²=0.40 and gap=0.02 - the former is memorising.

Rules applied

Gap-adjusted: score = R²_test − 0.5 · max(0, |gap| − 0.1). Penalises overfit beyond a 10-pt tolerance.

One-SE rule: among models within 0.05 R² of the top, pick the smallest |gap|.

Pareto frontier: models not dominated on (R²_test ↑, |gap| ↓).

RMSE and MAE are shown alongside R² for each rule's winner - they carry the same ranking as R² within a target but are operationally interpretable (mg/L for BOD/COD/TSS, pH units for pH).

Headline

Naive winner differs from gap-adjusted / one-SE winner in 5 / 8 global target rows and 55 / 304 per-experiment rows.

Global best per target (across all experiments)

All rules agree, gap ≤ 0.25All rules agree, gap > 0.25 - cautionGap-adj picks a different model (blue = recommended)

Click ⓘ on any Gap-adj winner for the reasoning behind that selection.

Source: 1880 model evaluations across 40 experiments.

2025 Residual Decomposition by Operational Regime

Decomposes 2025 residuals for the Phase 9 Voting (ElNet+RF+XGB) model across four operational axes: Flow quartile, Weekday vs Weekend, Season (DJF/MAM/JJA/SON), and Inlet Load quartile. Quartile thresholds are fit on the training set (2021-2024) only. Headline finding: error concentrates in Q4 high-flow and DJF winter regimes (2-3× overall MAE) for every BOD/TSS target - predictions during hydraulic or thermal stress should be flagged as low-confidence.

Model: predicted_Voting_run_2 (Phase 9 Voting = ElNet+RF+XGB). Quartile thresholds fitted on training years (2021-2024) and applied to 2025. Cells where MAE is >1.75× baseline are red - error concentrates in that regime. Bias coloured when |bias| > 0.5 × overall MAE (systematic over/under-prediction).

Comp COD - Persistent Failure Diagnostic

Effluent COD (Composite) is the only target where no model generalises across any experiment or phase (best Test R² = 0.154 across 38 experiment variants). Phase 9 Voting MAE doubled from 8.7 mg/L (2024 in-sample) to 18.1 mg/L (2025 test). Variance is not collapsed (σ-ratio = 0.78), which rules out the low-variance artefact that drives negative R² in Comp pH and Comp TSS. This section documents the distribution shift, the performance panorama across every experiment, and the evidence for a 2025 process change as the likely root cause.

Global best Test R² across all experiments: 0.154. Every regularisation strategy, feature set expansion, and engineering approach has been tried; none breaks through to meaningful generalisation on 2025.

Root Cause Analysis

1. Genuine 2025 process change - not a modelling artefact. MAE doubled (8.7 → 18.1 mg/L) while σ-ratio = 0.78, confirming the 2025 test set has comparable variance to training. The model is not hitting a low-variance wall - it is predicting the wrong values. This is the signature of a non-stationarity in the plant's COD removal mechanism that was absent during the 2021-2024 training window.

2. Inlet COD is a weak proxy for effluent COD under the current conditions. Composite effluent COD depends on secondary treatment efficiency, which is governed by MLSS, SVI, and aeration conditions - features with 35-50% missingness on composite rows (CONSIDER tier). When these are absent, the model falls back to inlet + flow, whose MI with effluent Comp COD is low (<0.15 on training data).

3. Small composite sample size amplifies memorisation. Composite targets have only ~515-633 training rows after dropna. With 17-32 features the effective rows-per-feature ratio is 16-37 - below the commonly cited heuristic of 50. Even heavily regularised models (ElNet α=10) risk encoding 2022-era COD spikes that do not recur in 2025.

4. Feature engineering and temporal lags do not help. Phase 10 full FE: best R² = −0.008 (Ridge). Phase 10b selective FE: best = −0.051. Phase 11 temporal lags: best = +0.107 (ElNet, Phase 11) but 2025 MAE remains ~17 mg/L. Every additional feature increases the risk of learning training-specific patterns without improving generalisation.

5. Recommended next steps. (a) Flag Comp COD predictions as unreliable in any operational dashboard until 2025 data is incorporated into training. (b) Collect causal features with higher priority: secondary sludge age (SRT), MLSS, and effluent turbidity have high missingness now but directly govern COD removal - their availability on composite measurement days would be the single highest leverage improvement. (c) Retrain once ≥ 90 new 2025 rows are available (roughly 3 months of composite measurements) and test whether the new regime is stable enough for a combined 2021-2025 model. (d) As an interim measure, a shallow decision tree (max depth 3) trained only on 2024-2025 data is likely to outperform any model trained on the full 2021-2024 window for near-term operational use.
